messages.content: Hypothetical - Children's Film Foundation Film -(Serial)  1972 - The Secret Garden ( Adapted from the Book) .

While the Children's Film Foundation (CFF) did produce a number of films for young audiences, as of my knowledge cutoff in 2023, there's no direct record of them producing a serial adaptation of "The Secret Garden" in 1972. The CFF was a British organization and non-profit which made films for children from the end of World War II until the late 1980s. They produced over 400 films during that period.

However, I can describe what a hypothetical CFF serial film adaptation of "The Secret Garden," made in 1972, might have been like, considering the style of CFF productions and the typical adaptations of novels during that time.

Title: "The Secret Garden" (1972)

Episodes: Given it’s a serial, it would likely be divided into multiple parts, perhaps 10-15 minute episodes released weekly. 

Plot Outline: The serial would closely follow the original story written by Frances Hodgson Burnett. It would tell the story of Mary Lennox, a young British girl born in India to wealthy parents who never wanted her. After being orphaned by a cholera outbreak, she is sent back to England to live with her uncle, Archibald Craven, whom she has never met, at his secluded manor in Yorkshire. There, Mary discovers a neglected and hidden garden that she sets about restoring. Along the way, she befriends a local boy named Dickon and her sickly cousin Colin, and the three of them grow and thrive alongside the garden they cultivate together.

Casting: The casting would feature child actors leading the role, with adults in the supporting roles. The role of Mary Lennox might be given to a rising British child star of the time, while the roles of Dickon and Colin would also be played by child actors. The tradition in CFF productions was to cast relatively unknown actors, so it would not have the star power of major Hollywood productions.

Production Style: The series would likely be shot on location in the UK, incorporating the picturesque countryside settings and historic manors to give an authentic Edwardian vibe. Due to budget constraints typical of CFF productions, the film would rely more on practical effects and real locations rather than elaborate sets or special effects. The garden would likely be a real garden dressed and managed to change across the production to show its transformation.

Target Audience: Consistent with CFF’s mission, the film would be made suitable for all children, with a focus on family-friendly themes, educational content, and a moral lesson embedded in the narrative. Themes such as friendship, healing, and the transformative power of nature would be emphasized.

Release and Format: The serial would be shown either in cinemas as part of Saturday matinees aimed at children or possibly on a children's television slot on the BBC, which would be typical of the time. Each episode would end with a small cliffhanger to entice viewers to return for the next part of the story.

It's important to remember that this is a hypothetical scenario. In real history, the CFF did not adapt "The Secret Garden" in 1972. However, the novel has had numerous adaptations, including film and television versions. For instance, the BBC has produced several adaptations, including TV series in 1975 and 1987, which are just two of the many adaptations done over the years.

Hypothetical Children’s Film and Television Foundation Film   (Made in 1986 for the BBC) - (Runtime 60 mins) -   "Don't shoot me I'm just an Invader!" -   A Space Invader, takes a teenage player inside the video game to explain it's culture, and why blindly shooting isn;t always the best course...

Title: "Don't Shoot Me, I'm Just an Invader!"

Release Date: 1986

Produced by: Children’s Film and Television Foundation in collaboration with the BBC

Runtime: 60 minutes

Plot Summary: The film opens in a bustling 1980s arcade where our protagonist, Jamie, a British teenager, is deeply engrossed in the new wave of arcade video games, particularly “Space Invaders.” Jamie is known for his high scores and dedication to defeating the pixelated invaders.

After a particularly intense session at the arcade, Jamie encounters a peculiar malfunction in the machine. Instead of the usual 'Game Over' screen, the message reads "Do you dare to understand us?" Intrigued and thinking this is a secret level, Jamie presses the "Yes" button, only to be suddenly transported into the digital world of the game.

Inside the game, Jamie meets an actual Space Invader named Zorgon, who is surprisingly friendly and articulate. Zorgon reveals that their invasion is not a warlike endeavor, but rather a desperate measure to find help for their dying planet.

As Jamie is taken through various “levels” of their world, he experiences their culture, understands their peaceful nature, and learns about the environmental catastrophe they're trying to escape. This animated portion of the film would blend traditional 2D animation with early CGI to illustrate the video game world and give kids a vibrant, eye-catching depiction of Zorgon's society.

Themes: The film's message centers around the importance of understanding those who are different from us, and not judging or attacking prematurely. It shows the consequences of violence and the value in seeking peaceful resolutions. As well, it touches on environmental themes, illustrating the impacts of neglecting one’s home planet and the universal responsibility of caring for our environment.

Cast: An up-and-coming British teen actor would take the role of Jamie, and voice actors would be used for Zorgon and other digital characters. The rest of the cast would include Jamie's friends, family, and other arcade-goers.

Production Style: The Earth scenes would be filmed on location in the UK, capturing the nostalgic feel of a 1980s arcade. The in-game scenes would be a mix of live-action (for Jamie's character) and early computer-generated imagery, mimicking the aesthetics of "Space Invaders" for Zorgon's world.

Target Audience: Keeping with the CFF’s ethos, the film would be suitable for children and families, utilizing fantastical elements and adventure to entertain while conveying its messages.

Release and Format: The film would likely be aired on the BBC as a television special, possibly during a holiday slot when family viewing was at its peak. Knowing the CFF’s focus on educational and moral tales, the film would probably be accompanied by educational materials for schools or discussion guides for parents to talk to their children about the themes presented.

Impact: If "Don't Shoot Me, I'm Just an Invader!" had been produced, it could have been an innovative piece combining children's programming with the emerging digital culture fostered by the video game industry. It might have been remembered as ahead of its time in addressing the relationship between video games and their players, and in advocating for understanding over violence—a topic that has grown increasingly relevant in subsequent decades.
Possibly an early role for David Tennant(given the 1986 date)

The hypothetical scenario of having a young David Tennant feature in a 1986 Children's Film and Television Foundation film for the BBC is an interesting consideration. In 1986, Tennant would have been around 15 years old and possibly at the start of his career, as he didn't graduate from the Royal Scottish Academy of Music and Drama until 1991.

If David Tennant had been cast in "Don't Shoot Me, I'm Just an Invader!" at that age, it could be imagined as one of his very early roles, setting the stage for his later, more prominent career in television and film.

If Tennant played Jamie, the teenage protagonist of the story, his character would have been a typical '80s teenager - possibly with a wild hairstyle and a wardrobe featuring the era's fashion of brightly colored clothing and classic high-top sneakers. As Jamie, Tennant would portray a skilled video game player who is thrust into an extraordinary situation when he is transported into the game world.

Tennant's natural charisma and ability to convey genuine emotion would make him an excellent choice for the role, allowing audiences to connect with Jamie's transformation from an initially competitive gamer to a compassionate individual who learns the importance of understanding and empathy.

Tennant's involvement in the film might have garnered it more attention and possibly made it a remembered piece of children's television, especially considering his later fame. The themes of the film would resonate well with the issues Tennant often tackles in his roles - most notably, during his time as the Doctor in "Doctor Who," where his character frequently advocates for understanding and peaceful solutions in the face of conflict.

Despite this being a hypothetical scenario, it's a fun consideration to think about how early roles can shape an actor's career trajectory and the potential impact of their performance on the themes and success of a children's film aimed at delivering a meaningful message.

Hypothetical Children’s Film and Television Foundation Film (Made in 1987 for the BBC) - (Runtime 60 mins) - Period Drama -  " The Wolves" - An Edwardian Middle class family is disrupted by two vistors claiming to be long lost aunts, but 's seem more interested in observing the porcelain and sivlerware than family interaction ..

Title: "The Wolves"

Release Date: 1987

Produced by: Children’s Film and Television Foundation in collaboration with the BBC

Runtime: 60 minutes

Plot Summary: Set in the peaceful backdrop of Edwardian England, "The Wolves" tells the story of the Middletons, a middle-class family whose quiet life is disrupted by the unexpected arrival of two mysterious women claiming to be long lost aunts from a distant branch of the family. The Allerton sisters, Eleanor and Beatrice, are charming and sophisticated, but their overbearing interest in the family's valuable heirlooms—fine porcelain and ornate silverware—soon raises suspicions.

As days pass, the family's eldest child, 14-year-old Thomas, notices odd happenings around the house: objects slightly moved, whispers behind closed doors, and the 'aunts' constantly asking questions about the family's finances and social connections. Thomas, along with his resourceful younger sister, Catherine, decides to investigate the true intentions of these so-called relatives.

Themes: The film would imbue a sense of mystery and adventure while addressing the themes of trust, family ties, and the dangers of deception. There would likely be an underlying message about being cautious with strangers and the importance of family unity in the face of adversity.

Cast: The cast would include a mix of seasoned British actors for the roles of the adults, especially the mysterious Allerton sisters, with the family’s children likely portrayed by rising stars in the children's acting scene.

Production Style: "The Wolves" would be made using period-appropriate costumes and settings to capture the Edwardian era's sophistication and elegance. The Middletons' household would be a significant location, with detailed set design reflecting the status and taste of a middle-class family of the time.

Target Audience: In line with CFF's focus, the film would be aimed at children and families, with enough suspense to keep viewers engaged but without any content deemed too frightening or inappropriate for younger viewers.

Release and Format: The film would likely air on the BBC as a family television special, perhaps in a prime Sunday evening slot, when dramas of this kind often attracted large viewerships. Schools might also use it as an educational tool to discuss the historical period and moral lessons the story presents.

Impact: Had "The Wolves" been produced, it might be remembered as a charming period piece that introduced young audiences to Edwardian culture and social norms while weaving in a captivating narrative about family and discernment. It could also serve as an early introduction to the genre of mystery and drama for young viewers, setting a precedent for children's programming that goes beyond education and entertainment to include suspense and intrigue.

Finally, "The Wolves" might also include a subtle commentary on class and the covetous nature of societal outsiders. By the conclusion, the film would likely reveal the true colors of the 'aunts,' with a satisfying resolution that reinforces the value of honesty and familial protection.

Hypothetical Children’s Film and Television Foundation Film (Made in 1988 for the BBC) - (Runtime 60 mins) -  "Pregeneration"-   A classroom of  very young Time Lords learning from a very famous Time Lady - Susuan (Carol Ann Ford).      Doctor Who meets Grange Hill.

Title: "Pregeneration"

Release Date: 1988

Produced by: Children’s Film and Television Foundation in collaboration with the BBC

Runtime: 60 minutes

Plot Summary: “Pregeneration” is set in the hallowed halls of the Time Lord Academy on the distant planet Gallifrey. The story focuses on a classroom of precocious young Time Lords, each with bright futures and potentially centuries of adventures ahead of them. These children are the elite of Gallifreyan society, destined to travel time and space, uphold the principles of the Time Lords, and explore the vastness of the universe.

The central character is a revered Time Lady, Susan (played by Carol Ann Ford, reprising her original role as the Doctor's granddaughter), who, after many journeys across time and space, has returned to her home planet to impart her wisdom to the next generation. The narrative would be woven around a special project the students undertake, which involves them learning about the great responsibility that comes with their unique gifts and the non-interference policy the Time Lords are supposed to uphold.

The students would grapple with moral dilemmas, scientific quandaries, and the philosophy behind time travel. As an homage to Ford's role as Susan, the script could cleverly incorporate references to her adventures with the Doctor, subtly connecting the show to "Doctor Who" lore without disrupting the standalone nature of the film.

Themes: Leveraging the Doctor Who universe to ground the story, "Pregeneration" would naturally cover themes such as responsibility, the consequences of meddling with time, ethical decision-making, and the need for compassion and understanding in all actions.

Cast: Carol Ann Ford would provide a strong link to the established "Doctor Who" series, delivering gravitas to the role of the educator. The young Time Lords would be played by a diverse group of talented child actors, each embodying the potential of Gallifrey's finest.

Production Style: The production would aim to capture the grandiosity of Gallifreyan architecture with an extensive set design, along with the use of special effects to depict technology far beyond our current capabilities. The costumes would reflect the otherworldly elegance of Time Lord society.

Target Audience: While firmly rooted in the world of "Doctor Who," the narrative of "Pregeneration" would also draw from the school drama genre, appealing to fans of both "Doctor Who" and shows like "Grange Hill." It would be accessible and engaging for children, with layers of complexity to also attract older fans of "Doctor Who."

Release and Format: The film would be broadcast as a special event on the BBC, perhaps geared towards the children's programming slot but with strong cross-generational appeal due to its connection with "Doctor Who."

Impact: "Pregeneration" would be an innovative blend of science fiction and school drama, potentially serving as a launching point for discussions about morality, the concept of time, and the importance of respecting history and the future. It might also inspire a sense of wonder and curiosity about the universe and our place within it.

If produced, the film could become a beloved cult classic among "Doctor Who" enthusiasts, appreciated for its unique take on the lore and educational value, much as "The Sarah Jane Adventures" would later be. A sophisticated storyline with an educational slant would fit squarely within the CFF’s remit to entertain and enlighten young audiences.
In further nods to Doctor Who,  you could also have Romanna and Leela  appears as potential instructors at the Academy?

Indeed, incorporating Romana and Leela as potential instructors at the Academy would provide a rich tapestry of "Doctor Who" lore to "Pregeneration," enticing fans of the series with appearances from beloved characters. The characters would offer different perspectives based on their backgrounds and adventures, enriching the educational experience for the young Time Lords.

Romana, who has been both a companion to the Doctor and a President of Gallifrey in the extended "Doctor Who" universe, would be especially fitting as an academy instructor, given her vast experience and Time Lord heritage. Known for her intelligence and decorum, she could provide instruction on Gallifreyan politics, temporal theory, or even ethics. Her character would command respect and admiration from the students, serving as a role model for what a Time Lord can achieve.

Leela, on the other hand, offers a contrast to the typical Gallifreyan. Despite being a human from a warrior tribe, her time with the Doctor and later as a resident of Gallifrey (as explored in various extended universe materials like audio dramas) gives her a unique insight into the practicalities of surviving in different times and worlds. She could teach classes on survival, the importance of different cultures, or adapting to unforeseen circumstances, providing a more hands-on approach to learning.

Each character would bring their own unique experiences to the classroom, helping to shape the minds of the fledgling Time Lords. Leela’s instinctual and forthright nature paired with Romana's diplomatic and intellectual approach would provide diverse teaching styles and viewpoints. This would not only make for engaging storytelling but also underline one of "Doctor Who's" core tenets: the value of different perspectives and the collaborative strength derived from them.

With these characters involved, "Pregeneration" would offer young viewers role models of intelligence, resilience, and adaptability, all wrapped up in an accessible and exciting narrative connected to the "Doctor Who" universe. Each episode could hint at or include lessons that are subtly drawn from the characters' past adventures with the Doctor, making every installment a treat for fans and an engaging story for new viewers.

Hypothetical Children’s Film and Television Foundation Film (Made in 1985 for Television South West/ITV)  - Historical Drama  "Benbow" -   Tom lives above an old Inn, where his parents earn a living..   however Tom hears tales of lamps on the Headland,  and movements in the church crypt..   One moonless night he uncovers more than a ghost that walks, as he may be "asked to watch the wall as the gentleman go by"..  (Despite the name the inspiration of Moonfleet, not  Treasure Island).

Title: "Benbow"

Release Date: 1985

Produced by: Children’s Film and Television Foundation for Television South West/ITV

Runtime: Approximately 60 minutes

Plot Summary: "Benbow" revolves around a young boy named Tom, who lives with his parents above the old Benbow Inn nestled in a small coastal village in Dorset. The inn, named after the famous Admiral John Benbow, is popular with locals and visitors, who often share tales of the sea and buried treasure, much like the stories found in "Moonfleet" by J. Meade Falkner, rather than Robert Louis Stevenson's "Treasure Island."

In the village, whispers speak of strange lamps seen on the headland and unusual sounds emerging from the church crypt late at night. Tom is captivated by these tales, his imagination fueled by stories of smuggling that date back to the bygone days of skullduggery on the English coast.

Curiosity gets the better of Tom one moonless night when he decides to investigate the headland himself. There, he discovers that the ghostly lights are not spirit manifestations but signals used by a group of smugglers—the "gentlemen"—who are part of a much larger network that sifts through the coastal waters, moving their illicit goods under the cover of darkness.

Themes: The narrative of "Benbow" explores themes of adventure, curiosity, and the ethical dilemmas faced when distinguishing right from wrong in a world full of shades of gray. It also touches on the notion of community and the secrets that can bind or divide its members.

Cast: Tom would be played by a bright-eyed young actor capable of portraying both the wide-eyed innocence of a child and the courage to face the dangers of the unknown. His parents and the colorful locals filling the inn would be portrayed by character actors who can bring warmth and authenticity to this historical setting.

Production Style: This historical drama would be filmed on location in the coastal regions of South West England, with cinematography capturing the rugged charm of the coastline and the quaint, cobbled streets of an English village. Period costumes and set designs would faithfully recreate the mid-18th to early 19th century, immersing viewers in the bygone era.

Target Audience: In keeping with the mission of the CFF, "Benbow" would be made accessible for children, allowing them to learn about an exciting part of their national history through a tale of adventure and moral consequence while also appealing to a family audience.

Release and Format: Intended as a television special for ITV, "Benbow" would likely be broadcast on an early evening slot, capturing the imaginations of young viewers and their parents alike.

Impact: As a children's historical drama, "Benbow" would not only entertain but also educate its audience about England's smuggling past, resonating with the same kind of intrigue that makes classic stories like "Moonfleet" endure. It would provide a portrayal of rural English life that is both romanticized and grounded in the realities of its time, with a balance of suspense and historical authenticity that could make it a classic within children's programming.

The line "asked to watch the wall as the gentlemen go by" pays homage to the poem "A Smuggler's Song" by Rudyard Kipling, which could serve as a thematic framework for the film, hinting at the secret world Tom discovers and the moral choices he faces as he grows up in the shadow of the smugglers' legacy.

Hypothetical Children’s Film and Television Foundation Film (Made in 1984 with Euston films) - Contemporary Thriller (100 mins) "Running Scared" Adapted from the book by Bernard Ashley.  (With the visual look Euston Films was known for on classic  TV like The professionals, The Sweeny etc. but age appropriate to the adaptation.)

Title: "Running Scared"

Release Date: 1984

Produced by: Children’s Film and Television Foundation with Euston Films

Runtime: 100 minutes

Plot Summary: "Running Scared" is an adaptation of the novel by Bernard Ashley, repackaged into a contemporary thriller for a young audience. The film follows the story of Paul, a London schoolboy whose life is upended when he witnesses a violent crime. Unable to shake what he saw and now fearing for his own safety, as the perpetrators want to silence him, Paul is thrust into a harrowing game of cat and mouse across the city.

Set against the gritty backdrop of London in the 1980s, Paul must navigate both the physical and psychological landscape of fear and uncertainty. As authorities fail to provide the protection he needs, Paul decides to go on the run, using his wits and the help of his friends to evade the dangerous elements that follow him.

The screenplay would work to maintain the intensity and suspense of the original novel while ensuring its suitability for a younger audience, staying true to the insightful and realistic portrayal of a child's perspective in a crisis.

Themes: Central themes of "Running Scared" would include the loss of innocence, the impact of crime and violence, resilience, trust, and the experience of urban life from a child's point of view.

Cast: The protagonist, Paul, would be played by a skilled young actor capable of carrying the film with a sense of authenticity and vulnerability. He would be surrounded by a strong supporting cast including family members, friends who become accomplices in his escape, and the intimidating figures pursuing him.

Production Style: True to the visual style Euston Films was known for, "Running Scared" would be shot on location, capturing the dark, moody streets of London and using the city as a character in itself. The cinematography would emphasize the tension and peril of Paul's situation, with handheld camera work to create a sense of immediacy and a gritty, almost neo-noir aesthetic aligned with the urban setting.

Target Audience: Made for a youth audience within CFF's guidelines, the film would be designed to be an engaging thriller that still respects the sensibilities of young viewers. It would be tense but not graphic, thrilling but not traumatizing, in order to convey the seriousness of the story's circumstances without crossing into adult content.

Release and Format: As a television film, "Running Scared" would likely premiere on an early evening slot suitable for family viewing, possibly as part of a special event or series highlighting the work of the CFF and Euston Films.

Impact: The film would aim to resonate with young audiences by portraying a protagonist in a situation that defies his age, allowing viewers to contemplate their own ideas of bravery, morality, and survival. Produced with the signature flair of Euston Films but tailored to a younger demographic, "Running Scared" could stand as a memorable entry in children’s thriller programming—serious in tone, stylish in presentation, and sensitive to its audience's age and experience.

Hypothetical Children’s Film and Television Foundation Film (Made in 1983 with Granada Television) (60 mins) -  A class of school children contemplate possible futures in a grim Merseyside under change.

Title: "Merseyside Dreams"

Release Date: 1983

Produced by: Children’s Film and Television Foundation in collaboration with Granada Television

Runtime: 60 minutes

Plot Summary: "Merseyside Dreams" is a poignant and thought-provoking drama set amidst the industrial transformation of early 1980s Merseyside, a region undergoing significant economic and social change. The film's plot centers around a class of school children in their final year before moving on to secondary education. As the city around them grapples with the closure of shipyards, challenges in the labor market, and community upheaval, the children's dreams for the future reflect a blend of hope, uncertainty, and the stark reality of their surroundings.

Their teacher, Mr. Evans, utilizes a unique classroom project to encourage the students to imagine their futures. He asks them to write essays and create presentations about where they see themselves in twenty years, understanding that the exercise is both aspirational and grounding against the backdrop of the shifting tides of Merseyside’s society.

As the kids work on the assignment, the film takes us through various vignettes of their lives at home and in their community, showcasing the diverse range of experiences and expectations that shape their outlook on life.

Themes: The film explores themes like ambition, resilience, and the impact of socio-economic factors on the life paths of individuals. It touches upon the importance of education, community support, and personal determination in overcoming adversity.

Cast: The children in the class would be played by a diverse ensemble of young actors, each portraying different backgrounds and personalities, offering a tapestry of working-class life. Mr. Evans would be a cornerstone character—perhaps played by a respected British character actor who could bring warmth and inspiration to the mentor role.

Production Style: Filmed on location in Merseyside, the cinematography of "Merseyside Dreams" would capture both the grit and the beauty of the region, documenting the urban landscape, the docks, the terraced streets, and the strong sense of community. The production design would be rooted in authenticity, reflecting the economic difficulties of the time.

Target Audience: Aligned with CFF principles, this film would aim to be accessible and relevant to children and families, particularly those from similar communities facing change. The drama would seek to teach young viewers about the social transitions occurring in post-industrial Britain while still offering an engaging narrative.

Release and Format: As a sixty-minute television film, "Merseyside Dreams" would likely broadcast on ITV in a prime after-school or weekend slot, appealing to a wide demographic with a special resonance for viewers in the North West of England.

Impact: This introspective piece would serve as a historical snapshot of the era, providing future generations with a lens into the lives of children growing up during a time of significant societal shift. By blending social realism with a message of hope and self-determination, "Merseyside Dreams" could become a testament to the resilience of the human spirit in the face of change and a reminder to young viewers that their aspirations need not be limited by the circumstances of their birth.
